title: Performance analyses of sensible heat storage systems for thermal applications
abstract:
INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF ENERGY RESEARCH, VOL. 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
PERFORMANCE ANALYSES OF SENSIBLE HEAT STORAGE
SYSTEMS FOR THERMAL APPLICATIONS
IBRAHIM DINCER*, SADIK DOST AND XIANGUO LI
Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Victoria, Victoria, B.C., Canada V8W 3P6
SUMMARY
In this paper, sensible heat storage (SHS) systems and performance evaluation techniques are studied. A detailed
investigation is presented of the availability of SHS techniques for solar thermal applications, selection criteria for SHS
systems, the economics of SHS systems, the main issues in evaluating SHS systems, the viability of SHS systems, the
environmental impacts of SHS systems and criteria for SHS feasibility studies, as well as energy saving options. In
addition to energy and exergy analyses, several deﬁnitions of energy and exergy eﬃciency for the performance of SHS
systems are provided with an illustrative example. ( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
No. of Figures: 4
No. of Tables: 2
No. of References: 14
KEY WORDS thermal energy storage; sensible heat storage; energy and exergy analyses; energy savings; system
economics; system viability; environmental impacts
1. INTRODUCTION
Energy storage is critically important to the success of any intermittent energy source in meeting demand.
This problem is particularly severe for solar energy because storage is needed most when the solar
availability is the lowest, namely in winter. There are several energy storage options as follows:
(1) hydrostorage;
(2) chemical batteries;
(3) ﬂywheels;
(4) chemical storage;
(5) compressed air storage;
(6) biological storage; and
(7) thermal storage.
Among these storage methods, thermal energy storage (TES), which has been considered an advanced
energy technology method, is of great importance in a wide variety of energy applications (Dincer and Dost,
1996). Certainly, TES is of particular interest and signiﬁcance in using this essential technique for solar
thermal applications such as heating, hot water, cooling, air-conditioning, etc., because of its intermittent
nature. In these applications, a TES system must be able to retain the energy absorbed for at least a few days
in order to supply the energy needed on cloudy days when the energy input is low.
An advantage of an eﬃcient TES is the storage of solar energy. Although it may have been designed
primarily for this purpose, it is not restricted to it. It may be used to store surplus energy from power plants,
usually in the form of waste water, waste energy from air-conditioners, waste energy from industrial
*Correspondence to: Dr. Ibrahim Dincer, Department of Mechanical Engineering, KFUPM, Dhahran 31261, Saudi Arabia.
CCC 0363—907X/97/121157—15$17.00
Received 24 September 1996
( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Accepted 7 October 1996
 processes, and so on. It becomes a sort of energy sink into which we can throw any form of energy which is
not needed for the moment. Common storage like this may not be so applicable for small houses but would
be useful for large-scale central heating systems.
Thermal energy can be stored as sensible heat, latent heat or chemical energy (Dincer et al., 1996).
(a) Sensible heat storage systems (e.g. rock beds, water tanks, aquifers) commonly use rocks or water as the
storage medium and are available for short and long-term storage. Heat is stored by increasing the storage
medium temperature.
(b) Latent heat storage systems, which store energy in phase-change materials (e.g. salt hydrates and
organic substances) are used for short-term storage. The heat is stored when the material changes phase
from a solid to a liquid. Materials used for this purpose are called phase-change materials (PCMs). The use
of latent heat TES using PCMs is an eﬀective way to store thermal energy (solar energy, industrial waste
heat, oﬀ-peak electricity) and has the advantages of high storage capacity and recovery for almost constant-
temperature heat. Energy densities for the PCMs are greater than those for sensible heat storage, resulting
in smaller and lighter storage devices and lower storage losses. The relatively constant temperature of
storage can maximize collector eﬃciency and minimize storage heat loss. An ideal PCM must have the
following features: appropriate phase change temperature, high latent heat, low cost, ready availability,
non-toxicity and non-ﬂammability, uniform phase change characteristics, and long life under repeated phase
change.
(c) Thermochemical storage (e.g. inorganic substances) is a new concept being considered for solar thermal
applications. This storage technique, which involves chemical reactions, is still in the development stage and
considerably more work is required to make it operational and establish its utility, particularly for solar
applications.
The selection of the TES systems mainly depends on the storage period required (i.e. diurnal or seasonal),
economic viability, operating conditions, etc. In practice, many research and development activities related
to energy have been concentrated on eﬃcient energy use and energy savings, leading to energy conservation.
In this regard, TES systems appear to be one of the most attractive thermal applications.
Selection of the performance of a TES is not easy. Despite the existence of many technically and
economically successful TES systems in practice, no ARI or ASHRAE standards have been developed to
provide a basis for the rating and performance testing of these systems yet. Recently, increasing interest has
been focused on the performance evaluation and comparison of TES systems by means of the exergy analysis
technique. Accurate determination of these criteria is essential in order to attain eﬃcient and eﬀective use of
TES systems and optimum operating conditions. Limited studies on the energy analysis of TES systems from
the performance evaluation point of view have already been undertaken by, for example, Rosen (1987; 1992),
Rosen et al. (1988), Rosen and Hooper (1996), Krane and Krane (1992a; 1992b) and Taylor et al. (1991). In
this study, detailed information on SHS systems is provided, and the energy and energy analyses of SHS
systems for performance evaluation are presented.
2. SHS SYSTEMS
For SHS systems, the classiﬁcation given in Table 1 can be made.
As mentioned earlier, in the SHS method energy is stored by changing the temperature of the storage
medium (e.g. water, air, oil, rock beds, bricks, ceramic bricks, sand or soil). An SHS system consists of
a storage medium, a container and input/output devices. Containers must both retain storage material and
prevent loss of thermal energy. Thermal stratiﬁcation, i.e. a thermal gradient across the storage medium, is
desirable. Maintaining stratiﬁcation is much simpler in solid storage media than in ﬂuids. The amount of
energy stored by an SHS device is proportional to the diﬀerence between the storage input and output

investigation is presented of the availability of SHS techniques for solar thermal applications, selection criteria for SHS
systems, the economics of SHS systems, the main issues in evaluating SHS systems, the viability of SHS systems, the
environmental impacts of SHS systems and criteria for SHS feasibility studies, as well as energy saving options. In
addition to energy and exergy analyses, several deﬁnitions of energy and exergy eﬃciency for the performance of SHS
systems are provided with an illustrative example. ( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)

This problem is particularly severe for solar energy because storage is needed most when the solar
availability is the lowest, namely in winter. There are several energy storage options as follows:
Among these storage methods, thermal energy storage (TES), which has been considered an advanced

nature. In these applications, a TES system must be able to retain the energy absorbed for at least a few days
in order to supply the energy needed on cloudy days when the energy input is low.
An advantage of an eﬃcient TES is the storage of solar energy. Although it may have been designed
primarily for this purpose, it is not restricted to it. It may be used to store surplus energy from power plants,
usually in the form of waste water, waste energy from air-conditioners, waste energy from industrial

The selection of the TES systems mainly depends on the storage period required (i.e. diurnal or seasonal),
economic viability, operating conditions, etc. In practice, many research and development activities related
to energy have been concentrated on eﬃcient energy use and energy savings, leading to energy conservation.
In this regard, TES systems appear to be one of the most attractive thermal applications.
Selection of the performance of a TES is not easy. Despite the existence of many technically and
economically successful TES systems in practice, no ARI or ASHRAE standards have been developed to
provide a basis for the rating and performance testing of these systems yet. Recently, increasing interest has
been focused on the performance evaluation and comparison of TES systems by means of the exergy analysis
technique. Accurate determination of these criteria is essential in order to attain eﬃcient and eﬀective use of
TES systems and optimum operating conditions. Limited studies on the energy analysis of TES systems from
the performance evaluation point of view have already been undertaken by, for example, Rosen (1987; 1992),
Rosen et al. (1988), Rosen and Hooper (1996), Krane and Krane (1992a; 1992b) and Taylor et al. (1991). In
this study, detailed information on SHS systems is provided, and the energy and energy analyses of SHS
systems for performance evaluation are presented.
For SHS systems, the classiﬁcation given in Table 1 can be made.
As mentioned earlier, in the SHS method energy is stored by changing the temperature of the storage
medium (e.g. water, air, oil, rock beds, bricks, ceramic bricks, sand or soil). An SHS system consists of
a storage medium, a container and input/output devices. Containers must both retain storage material and
prevent loss of thermal energy. Thermal stratiﬁcation, i.e. a thermal gradient across the storage medium, is
desirable. Maintaining stratiﬁcation is much simpler in solid storage media than in ﬂuids. The amount of
energy stored by an SHS device is proportional to the diﬀerence between the storage input and output
Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Table 1. Storage media for SHS systems
temperatures, the mass of the storage medium, and the medium’s heat capacity. Each medium has its own
advantages and disadvantages. For example, water has approximately twice the heat capacity of rock
(2·326 kJ kg~1 C~1) and soil. The high heat capacity of water often makes tanks of water a logical choice for
TES systems that operate in the temperature range needed for building heating or cooling. The relatively low
heat capacity of rocks and ceramics (&0·84 kJ kg~1 C~1) is somewhat oﬀset by the large temperature
changes possible with these materials and their relatively high density.
Annual storage systems include aquifers, which are water-bearing rocks found near or at nominal depths
below the Earth’s surface. These are large geological formations that can be tapped by wells. Although some
technical problems (i.e. biofouling and clogging of pipes and wells) remain to be solved, aquifers oﬀer
potentially very low-cost storage for large-scale systems. Except for the cost of access wells, the aquifer
container is almost free. However, rather low round-trip aquifer storage eﬃciencies (&70%) may necessitate
their use only in conjunction with such low-cost energy supplies as industrial waste heat. Wells have been
drilled to transport water to underground aquifers (underground areas of water-bearing rock, sand or
gravel). Well spacing, depth and size are functions of the aquifer. Aquifer systems have received worldwide
attention because of their potential for large-scale and long-term SHS.
Solar ponds, which are both low-temperature solar collectors and long-term storage devices, trap solar
energy and store it in the bottom layer as there is no convective loss through the salt gradient part of the
pond. Salt-gradient solar ponds may be economically attractive in climates with little snow and in areas
where land is readily available. In addition, sensible cooling storage can be added to existing facilities by
creating a small pond or lake on the site. In some installations, this can be done as part of property
landscaping. Cooling takes place by surface evaporation and the rate of cooling can be increased with a water
spray or fountain. Ponds can be used as an outside SHS system or as a means of rejecting surplus heat from
refrigeration or process equipment.
Rock is another good SHS material from the standpoint of cost, but its capacity is only about half that
of water. The rock storage bin used in home air-heating systems is practical (Dincer and Dost, 1996).
The advantage of rock over water is that it can easily be used for SHS above 100°C. Rock beds and
water storage are two simple systems that can be utilized in many ways. They may be used in conjunction
with heat pumps to improve the eﬃciency of heat recovery, or with more elaborate heat exchangers, or
in conjunction with each other. Eldighidy (1991) presented a detailed and extensive theoretical and
experimental investigation of solar storage tanks with SHS and indicated that water, rock beds and
PCMs are the most suitable media for solar TES applications. Three diﬀerent conﬁgurations of SHS tanks
(Figure 1) for solar applications were examined. The most common heat storage system is a water ﬁlled
container in direct contact with both the solar collector and the house heating system (Figure 1(a)). The
coolest water from the bottom of the tank is circulated to the collector for solar heating and then returned to
the top of the tank. The warmest water from the top of the tank is circulated directly through baseboard
radiators or radiant heating panels inside the rooms. Figure 1(b) shows another system which consists of
a copper coiled-ﬁnned tube immersed in a tank of solar heated water. Rocks are the best known and most
widely used SHS medium for air collectors. An attractive method uses both water and rocks as storage media

using a heat exchanger to extract solar heat from a storage tank; (c) Harry Thomason’s technique using both water and stone as storage
(Figure 1(c)) which is known as Harry Thomason’s method. In this system, heated water from the solar
collector goes in at the top, sinks as it cools and ﬁnally leaves at the bottom to begin its circulation again to
the collectors. It is surrounded by a heap of river rock through which air is circulated to carry the heat into
the house; the entirety of the river rocks and water are contained within insulated walls. The advantage is that
the high heat capacity of the water and the extensive area of the inner container lead to an eﬃcient transfer of
SHS systems ﬁnd application possibilities in a range of capacities from only a few hours to seasonal storage.
An example of seasonal storage is the collection of solar energy available during the summer to be used for
winter heating. A number of practical limitations in terms of the space requirement and capital cost restrict
most SHS schemes from storage cycles of a few hours to a maximum of a few days. For example, consider
a building or process requiring 1000 kW of chilling capacity for 900 h of full load operation. The minimum
annual cooling energy that must be stored is 900]1000"900 000 kW h. If ice is used as the storage medium,
about 3 kg of ice is required to store 1 MJ of cooling, and hence the ice required is 9 720 000 kg or 9720 m3.
Assuming a perfect tank with no standby heat losses and no safety margin, a 2 m deep tank may cover an
Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
area the size of a football ﬁeld. A water storage system would be at least ten times larger. Costs for such large
systems are unacceptable. For this reason, greater attention has been paid to short-term or diurnal SHS
The economic justiﬁcation for SHS systems assumes that the annual income needed to cover capital and
operating costs will be less than that required for primary generating equipment supplying the same service
SHS systems are installed for two major reasons (Dincer et al., 1996a): (i) lower initial costs; and (ii) lower
operating costs. Lower initial costs are usually obtained when the load to meet is of short duration and there
is a long time gap before the load returns. Secondary capital costs may also be lower for thermal storage. For
example, the electrical service entrance size can sometimes be reduced because energy demand is lower. In
order to complete the economic analysis, the initial costs must be determined. Equipment costs should be
obtained from each of the manufacturers under consideration and an estimate of the installation cost made.
The cost savings along with the net capital costs should be analysed using the life-cycle-cost method, or other
suitable methods, to determine which system is best for the project.
Other items to be considered are space requirements and reliability of the system, and ease of interface
to the delivery system planned for the project. An optical energy storage application will maximize the
savings in utility charges and minimize the initial cost of the installation needed to achieve the savings.
Consequently, the decision to install a storage system must be based on anticipated system loads, load
characteristics, and generating capacity mix for a very extended period. Regional uncertainty about the
future economic outlook, life-style changes, or the availability of low-cost charging power for the storage
system may lead to diﬀering investment decisions if alternative technical solutions are feasible. Uncertainty
also stems from the technical dynamics of the alternative solutions to the problem that make SHS systems
The following parameters are of great importance and inﬂuence the viability of an SHS system:
(b) thermal and electrical load proﬁles;
(c) availability of waste or excess thermal energy;
(d) electrical costs and rate structures;
(e) type of thermal generating equipment; and
(f) building or plant type and occupancy.
2.4. Main issues for evaluating SHS systems
Since there are many factors that inﬂuence the selection, implementation and operation of an SHS system, it
is necessary that comprehensive feasibility study be developed. This study should take into consideration all
variables that impact evaluation of the true cost beneﬁts of a candidate SHS implementation. The following
main issues should be addressed before the implementation of an SHS system:
(a) management objectives (short- or long-term economics);
(b) ﬁnancial parameters of the project;
(c) available utility incentives;
(d) new or existing plant (existing plant would reduce its implementation cost);
(e) net storage capacity (e.g. peak-day load);
SENSIBLE HEAT STORAGE SYSTEM PERFORMANCE
( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
(f) utility rate schedule time-frames and energy charges;
(g) full or partial SHS system;
(h) SHS system options best suited for the application;
(i) anticipated operating strategies for each of the SHS system options;
(j) space available for the SHS system (e.g. tank);
(k) type of SHS system (open or closed);
(l) implementation logistics of the SHS under consideration;
(1) new or existing plant (if existing, the modiﬁcations required; if new, the system capacity);
(2) SHS system location and structural impact;
(3) heat exchanger requirements;
(5) automatic control requirements;
(6) new electrical service requirements.
2.5. SHS feasibility study for an existing facility
For an existing facility, the SHS feasibility study should address the following requirements (for a facility
under design, the requirements are generally the same, except that the database must be developed ﬁrst,
lowing to the non-existence of the actual operating data).
(1) the utility’s maximum incentive;
(2) the facility occupancy hours;
(3) the facility operating requirements;
(4) existing physical constraints;
(5) the facility peak-day load or monthly average requirements;
(6) historic energy consumption rates
(1) the types of SHS systems best suited for the particular application;
(2) general implementation logistics for each of the SHS systems under consideration;
(3) the plant’s yearly energy consumption with or without an SHS system;
(4) the SHS size utilization factor;
(5) the projected operating cost reduction for each of the SHS systems under consideration.
(2) a system implementation recommendation.
2.6. Energy savings by SHS systems
It is obvious that the main objective of SHS technology is to save energy and hence money. These savings can
be achieved in several manners (Dincer et al., 1997).
(a) The consumption of purchased energy can be reduced by storing waste or surplus thermal energy
available at certain times for use at other times. For example, solar energy can be stored during the day for
(b) The demand of purchased electrical energy can be reduced by storing electrically produced thermal
energy during oﬀ-peak periods to meet the thermal loads that occur during high demand periods. Increasing
Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
attention has been paid to the reduction of peak demand or transfer of energy loads from high to low
consumption periods. For example, an electric chiller can be used to charge a chilled water storage system at
night for reducing the electrical demand peaks usually experienced during the day.
(c) The purchase of additional equipment for heating, cooling or air-conditioning applications can be
deferred, and the equipment sizing in new facilities can be reduced. Equipment can be operated when thermal
loads are low to charge SHS systems. It is important to withdraw energy from storage to help meet the
maximum thermal loads that exceed equipment capacity.
2.7. Environmental impacts of SHS systems
SHS systems can contribute signiﬁcantly to meeting society’s needs for more eﬃcient, environmentally
benign energy use in building heating and cooling, space power and utility applications. The use of SHS
systems will result in some signiﬁcant beneﬁts to the general well-being of the environment. These beneﬁts are
(a) conservation of fossil fuels, by the switch to eﬃcient generating plant;
(b) reductions in carbon dioxide emissions; and
(c) reductions in CFC emissions.
2.8. Practical application of an SHS from the energy saving and environmental points of view
Here we present a recent application to show how SHS systems provide energy savings and reductions in
environmental pollutants. This system was introduced in the Netherlands by an insurance company, Anova
Verzekering, in its newly renovated oﬃce in Amersfoort (IEA, 1994). It was installed as an innovative space
conditioning system. A schematic diagram of the system for the heating and cooling cases is shown in
Figure 2. In winter, an electric heat pump is used to supply a hydronic heating and cooling distribution

system using heat from a warm ground-water aquifer as heat source. In the heating mode, the ground-water
is cooled to 8°C and stored in a second water layer. At the end of the heating season, suﬃcient cool water has
been stored to meet the oﬃce cooling needs in summer without requiring operation of the heat pump or any
other active cooling system. When cooling is needed, the cooled ground-water is pumped up and used to
extract heat from the hydronic system. It then returns to the warm well at temperatures of between 17 and
20°C, thus providing a useful source of stored heat. Very eﬃcient heat pump operation can be expected by
using a warm heat source, coupled with the use of a heat distribution temperature of 35 to 40°C. It must be
indicated that the key issue in the success of this design is the utilization of an advanced ceiling-mounted
hydronic heating and cooling distribution system. This system oﬀers signiﬁcant energy savings and environ-
mental beneﬁts as shown in Table 2. With a subsidy of US$ 212 000 from the Dutch government representing
over 20% of the total installation costs, the savings in energy costs will pay back the additional investment
costs within 6·5 years. This is relative to the conventional alternative technology of gas heating and electric
During the last decade, exergy analysis of TES has been recognized as a powerful method for performance
evaluations of TES systems by, for example, Beckman and Gilli (1984), Hahne (1986), Rosen et al., (1988) and
Taylor et al., (1991). In this section, we provide an energy and exergy analysis of SHS systems—for details, see
Rosen (1992), Rosen et al., (1988), Rosen and Hooper (1996) and Taylor et al. (1991).
Figure 3 shows a basic type of SHS system. This is a closed system storing heat in a ﬁxed amount of storage
ﬂuid B, to or from which heat is transferred through a heat exchanger by using a heat transport ﬂuid A. In the
present analyses, it is this type of SHS which is considered.

In an SHS system there are three main steps, namely charging, storing and discharging. These are taken
into account. An illustration of these steps in an overall SHS process considered is shown in Figure 4 (Rosen
et al., 1988). In order to simplify the operation of the heat exchangers, it is assumed that there are no heat
losses to the surrounding environment from the charging or discharging ﬂuids, all heat removed from the
charging ﬂuid is added to the storage medium, and, for the discharging period, all heat added to the
discharging ﬂuid originates in the storage medium. This assumption becomes valid for small rates of heat loss
from the charging and discharging ﬂuids and can be used as an approximation to actual performance if these
heat losses are included with heat losses from the SHS. As pointed out by Rosen (1991), in practical systems
the ﬂows of the charging and discharging ﬂuids are often steady and have time-dependent properties.
Under equations (1) and (2), individual energy and exergy balances can be obtained for the charging period

4. ENERGY AND EXERGY EFFICIENCIES
In thermal applications, the term eﬃciency is commonly used in two diﬀerent ways which have been denoted
energy eﬃciency (ﬁrst-law eﬃciency) and exergy eﬃciency (second-law eﬃciency). Energy eﬃciency, which
Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
merely reﬂects the standard laws of energy conservation, is deﬁned as the ratio of useful energy output to
total energy input, for any process. Exergy eﬃciency incorporates the notion of increasing thermodynamic
unavailability, as reﬂected by increasing entropy, in a process. However, it can be deﬁned consistently as
a ratio which is the minimum amount of available work theoretically required to accomplish the desired
result by the most eﬃcient possible process. The denominator is the actual quantity of available work
consumed. The energy eﬃciencies are always lower than the energy eﬃciencies, because the irreversibilities of
the process destroy some of the input exergy.

The energy and exergy analyses for simple closed-tank storage with heat transfers by a heat exchanger have
been obtained in a simple manner for practical SHS applications. A complete storing cycle is considered
along with the individual charging, storing and discharging periods. Various energy and exergy eﬃciency
deﬁnitions have been presented and these may prove useful in the development of valid and acceptable
standards for the performance evaluation and comparison of practical SHS systems. It is suggested that such
standards be based on exergy eﬃciencies rather than energy eﬃciencies. These energy and exergy eﬃciencies
depend on which quantities are considered to be products and which are considered to be inputs. As a result,
exergy eﬃciencies, which are a measure of how nearly a system approaches ideal performance taking into
account the loss of availability of heat in storage operations due to temperature reductions because of mixing,
are practically more meaningful and useful than energy eﬃciencies.
Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
( 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
The following is a simple example illustrating the nature of the energy and exergy eﬃciencies of SHS systems,
as a basis for assessment of the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent types of SHS.
(1) Consider two SHSs, X and Y, in surrounding environments at 25°C. Each receives 104 650 kJ of heat
from a stream of 500 kg of water, which is cooled from 75 to 25°C. That is,
Q*"m*c1 DT"500]4·186](75!25)"104 650 kJ
After 24 h, the heat of 94 185 kJ is recovered from storage system X by a stream of 4500 kg of water being
heated from 25 to 30°C. That is,
Q0"m0c1 DT"4500]4·186](30!25)"94 185 kJ
Therefore, the energy eﬃciency for storage system X becomes
On the other hand, storage system Y stores the heat for 100 days, after which an energy quantity of
94 185 kJ (Q0) is recovered by heating a stream of 500 kg of water from 25 to 70°C, with the resulting energy
eﬃciency of the storage cycle, gY"Q0/Q*"0·90. It is useful to mentioned that the ability of storing sensible
heat in a given tank (or container) strongly depends on the value for the material of oc1.
As a result, both storage systems have the same energy eﬃciency when calculated by considering the
ﬁrst-law of thermodynamics, but storage system Y, which stores the heat for 100 days rather than one day,
and which returns the heat at the much more useful temperature of 70 rather than 30°C, gives a clearly
superior performance. It is obvious that a more perceptive measure of comparison than that provided by the
energy eﬃciency of the storage cycle is needed if the true usefulness of an SHS is to be assessed and a rational
basis for the optimization of its economic value established. Therefore, it is necessary to introduce the exergy
eﬃciency which is a measure of the eﬀectiveness of the SHS system. An eﬃciency deﬁned simply as the
percentage of the total energy stored in a system which can be recovered ignores the quality (availability) of
the recovered energy, and so cannot provide a measure of ideal performance. This is mentioned above.
Obviously, the exergy eﬃciency deﬁnition provides a better measure.
(2) Consider the aforementioned example of SHSs X and Y. For these cases, the exergy eﬃciency can easily
be obtained. The exergy changes the charging and discharging periods are obtained as

Int. J. Energy Res., 21, 1157—1171 (1997)
It is clear that storage system Y has the advantage over storage system X, owing to the higher temper-
ature—and therefore greater availability—of its recoverable heat, when properly expressed.
SHS systems have an enormous potential for more eﬀective use of thermal energy equipment and for
facilitating large-scale energy substitutions from the perspectives of economy and environmental impact. In
this respect, SHS systems appear to be the only solution to correct the mismatch between supply and demand
of energy. They can contribute signiﬁcantly to meeting society’s needs for more eﬃcient, environmentally
benign energy use in various energy sectors. In this study, we have addressed the availability of SHS
techniques for solar thermal applications, the economics of SHS systems, storage size limitations, the main
issues for system evaluation, eﬀective parameters to measure system viability, the environmental impact of
SHS systems and energy saving options, and also provided energy and exergy analyses of SHS systems with
a practical example. The following issues can also be extracted from this study:
(1) the selection of the SHS systems mainly depends on the storage period required (e.g. diurnal or seasonal),
economic viability, operating conditions, etc.;
(2) the economic justiﬁcation for SHS systems assumes that the annual income needed to cover capital and
operating costs will be less than that required for primary generating equipment supplying the same
(3) the storage eﬃciency is the ratio of energy that can be withdrawn from storage to the amount put into
storage. Storage eﬃciencies up to 90% can be achieved in well-stratiﬁed water tanks that are fully
charged and discharged on a daily cycle;
(4) substantial energy savings can be realized by taking advantage of SHSs when implementing techniques
such as using waste energy and surplus heat, reducing electrical demand charges, and avoiding heating,
cooling or air-conditioning equipment purchases;
(5) SHS is presently identiﬁed as the most economical storage technology for building heating, cooling and
air-conditioning applications.
